perturbation.original_text: Section 11. Notices. All notices, requests, consents, demands and other communications provided for by this Agreement will be in writing and shall be deemed sufficient if delivered in person or by express courier to the party to be notified.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 11. Notices. All notices, requests, consents, demands and other communications provided for by this Agreement will be in writing and shall be deemed sufficient if delivered by regular mail to the party to be notified.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the delivery method to 'regular mail' may conflict with legal requirements for certain types of notices, where proof of receipt (e.g., certified mail) is necessary.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 5(b)(2)(C) discusses service by mail. The added language specifies regular mail only, eliminating options such as electronic service or other means that might be required or permitted in certain legal contexts, potentially conflicting with proper service requirements in litigation. Rule 5 also specifies other methods like consent to electronic means, which this change would not permit.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 5. Serving and Filing Pleadings and Other Papers | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 5. Serving and Filing Pleadings and Other Papers (a) Service: When Required. (1) In General. Unless these rules provide otherwise, each of the following papers must be served on every party: (A) an order stating that service is required; (B) a pleading filed after the original complaint, unless the court orders otherwise under Rule 5(c) because there are numerous defendants; (C) a discovery paper required to be served on a party, unless the court orders otherwise; (D) a written motion, except one that may be heard ex parte; and (E) a written notice, appearance, demand, or offer of judgment, or any similar paper. (2) If a Party Fails to Appear. No service is required on a party who is in default for failing to appear. But a pleading that asserts a new claim for relief against such a party must be served on that party under Rule 4 . (3) Seizing Property. If an action is begun by seizing property and no person is or need be named as a defendant, any service required before the filing of an appearance, answer, or claim must be made on the person who had custody or possession of the property when it was seized. (b) Service: How Made. (1) Serving an Attorney. If a party is represented by an attorney, service under this rule must be made on the attorney unless the court orders service on the party. (2) Service in General. A paper is served under this rule by: (A) handing it to the person; (B) leaving it: (i) at the person's office with a clerk or other person in charge or, if no one is in charge, in a conspicuous place in the office; or (ii) if the person has no office or the office is closed, at the person's dwelling or usual place of abode with someone of suitable age and discretion who resides there; (C) mailing it to the person's last known address—in which event service is complete upon mailing; (D) leaving it with the court clerk if the person has no known address; (E) sending it to a registered user by filing it with the court's electronic-filing system or sending it by other electronic means that the person consented to in writing—in either of which events service is complete upon filing or sending, but is not effective if the filer or sender learns that it did not reach the person to be served; or (F) delivering it by any other means that the person consented to in writing—in which event service is complete when the person making service delivers it to the agency designated to make delivery. (3) Using Court Facilities. [Abrogated (Apr._, 2018, eff. Dec. 1, 2018)] (c) Serving Numerous Defendants. (1) In General. If an action involves an unusually large number of defendants,

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text, by specifying 'regular mail,' contradicts FRCP 5(b)(2)(C), which states a paper is served 'by mailing it to the person's last known address—in which event service is complete upon mailing'. The original text allowed for methods like express courier, which inherently provide tracking or proof of delivery. The change to regular mail introduces uncertainty and potential legal challenges regarding proof of service, especially if receipt is disputed, potentially violating established legal procedure.

perturbation.original_text: Section 7. Notice of Unauthorized Use. The Receiving Party will notify the Disclosing Party promptly upon discovery of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information or any other breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party, and will reasonably cooperate with the Disclosing Party to regain possession of the Confidential Information and prevent its further unauthorized use or disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 7. Notice of Unauthorized Use. The Receiving Party will notify the Disclosing Party within 30 days of discovery of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information or any other breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party, and will cooperate with the Disclosing Party to regain possession of the Confidential Information and prevent its further unauthorized use or disclosure if the Disclosing Party compensates the Recipient for reasonable costs.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the notification to 30 days and adding a compensation contingency before cooperation may violate the duty to mitigate damages associated with trade secret misappropriation, and shifts the cost to the disclosing party.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA aims to protect trade secrets. Delaying notification of a breach and conditioning cooperation on compensation hinders the disclosing party's ability to promptly mitigate damages and recover misappropriated trade secrets, potentially undermining the protections afforded by the DTSA, specifically sections related to remedies and injunctions. Also, adding a compensation requirement before reasonably cooperating contradicts the intent of the Act to quickly prevent further damage.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 30-day delay in notifying the Disclosing Party of unauthorized use and conditions cooperation on compensation. This directly contradicts the duty to mitigate damages associated with trade secret misappropriation. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the perturbation explanation and law explanation clearly state that delaying notification and conditioning cooperation on compensation hinders the disclosing party's ability to promptly mitigate damages and recover misappropriated trade secrets, which undermines the protections afforded by law.

perturbation.original_text: Section 12. Miscellaneous. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York applicable to contracts wholly made and performed in such state.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 12. Miscellaneous. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California, without regard to its conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to California, especially without considering conflict of laws principles, may affect the enforceability of specific clauses if those clauses are valid under New York law but not under California law. This may affect the entire agreement's validity, since ADP is an LLC, and the contract says it is to be performed in New York.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Choice of Law Principles
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401 permits parties to a contract to choose New York law to govern the contract, even if it has no reasonable relation to the state, provided the agreement involves at least $250,000. By switching the governing law to California, the parties might lose the ability to have the agreement governed under NY law, as they initially intended, thus contradicting that statute's provisions regarding freedom to contract, potentially leading to a less favorable outcome than initially bargained for. If the value of the agreement is over $250,000, it contradicts the right to choose NY law.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 24-A General Obligations ARTICLE 5 Creation, Definition and Enforcement of Contractual Obligations TITLE 14 Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum In Certain Transactions up TITLE 14 Enforceability of Clauses Respecting Choice of Law and Choice of Forum In Certain Transactions next SECTION 5-1402 Choice of forum This entry was published on 2018-08-31 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-1401 Choice of law General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 14 § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking.

Choice of law Current as of January 01, 2024 | Updated by FindLaw Staff 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1-301 of the uniform commercial code , may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1-301 of the uniform commercial code . 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking. perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401, as evidenced by the scraped law snippets. The original contract specified New York law, but the modified contract switches the governing law to California. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, NY GOB § 5-1401 states that parties can agree to have New York law govern if the transaction is over $250,000, regardless of its relation to the state. By changing the governing law, the modified text potentially removes the parties' right to choose NY law, thus contradicting the statute. This creates a legal inconsistency, as the contract now defaults to California law, potentially undermining the initial agreement's enforceability under NY law.

perturbation.original_text: Section 3. Confidentiality Obligation. Except as required by law, the Receiving Party shall treat as confidential and will not use (other than for the purpose of evaluating the Partner Application as stated in Section 2 above), disclose or otherwise make available any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to any person other than Representatives of the Receiving Party who have a business need to know Mutual Non-­‐Disclosure Agreement Version 2 2 for purposes of the Evaluation, and who have been advised by the Receiving Party of the terms and conditions of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 3. Confidentiality Obligation. The Receiving Party shall treat as confidential and will not use (other than for the purpose of evaluating the Partner Application as stated in Section 2 above), disclose or otherwise make available any Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party to any person other than Representatives of the Receiving Party who have a business need to know for purposes of the Evaluation. The Receiving Party is not required to advise their Representatives of the terms and conditions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to advise representatives of the terms and conditions of the agreement weakens the confidentiality protection and creates a risk that Representatives unknowingly violate the agreement, which increases the risk of trade secret misappropriation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EEA criminalizes the theft of trade secrets. By not requiring representatives to be informed of the NDA's terms, the receiving party increases the risk of unintentional disclosure, which could be argued as a failure to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, indirectly enabling a violation of the EEA if a representative's actions lead to misappropriation. Not informing representatives weakens the protections of the confidential information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for the Receiving Party to advise their Representatives of the terms and conditions of the agreement. The Law Explanation states that not informing representatives weakens the protections of the confidential information, and increases the risk of unintentional disclosure, which could be argued as a failure to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. The scraped law snippet mentions the Economic Espionage Act of 1996, which broadly criminalizes the theft of trade secrets. By weakening the confidentiality protection, there's an increased risk of trade secret misappropriation, creating a legal contradiction.
